The company’s headquarters are located in Grenchen, Switzerland. Breitling is known for producing high-quality, precision watches for pilots, divers, and other professionals. The company’s watches are known for their durability, accuracy, and distinctive design, and are highly sought after by collectors and enthusiasts. In addition to its headquarters in Switzerland, Breitling has boutiques and authorized dealers located in cities around the world. Read More × Girard Perregaux The Girard-Perregaux watch brand was established in 1791, making it one of the oldest luxury watch manufacturers in Switzerland. The brand is well-known for its sophisticated watches as well as the technological improvements that it has made during its long history of invention and excellence in the watchmaking industry. During the course of its history, the brand is responsible for a number of significant advancements in horology, including the invention of the wristwatch and numerous other significant timekeeping advances. The timepieces produced by Girard-Perregaux are renowned for their unmatched elegance, outstanding craftsmanship, and meticulous attention to detail. Collectors and watch aficionados all around the globe have an insatiable appetite for the brand’s collections, which include everything from traditional dress watches to timepieces designed for sports and diving. Read More × Hublot Hublot is a luxury watch brand based in Switzerland. The company was founded in 1980 by Carlo Crocco. The brand is known for its bold, contemporary designs, and its use of advanced materials, such as carbon fiber and ceramic. Hublot is particularly well-known for its “Big Bang” collection, which features watches with a distinctive, layered case design, as well as its “Classic Fusion” collection, which is inspired by traditional watchmaking styles. Hublot is also known for its collaborations with other luxury brands and personalities, such as Ferrari, and Pelé, and for its commitment to traditional watchmaking techniques. Hublot watches are highly sought-after by collectors and enthusiasts for their unique style, and craftsmanship. Read More × Ice Watch Ice-Watch is a bold and accessible watch brand that emerged in the entry-level fashion timepiece segment. Founded by Jean-Pierre Lutgen in late 2006 and distributed globally since 2007, it fills the niche for colourful, stylish watches that feel both youthful and universally appealing. pages.content: Watchmaking – WatchDNA Skip to content BUILDING A COMMUNITY OF WATCH LOVERS WE AIM TO BECOME YOUR TRUSTED DIRECTORY FOR ALL THINGS WATCHES Item added to your cart View cart Check out Continue shopping Watchmaking Our goal is to create a platform where everyone that wants to learn about timepieces feels welcome. We are here to help you on your quest to discover all things watches. With the expertise of watchmakers in our community we have produced an easy-to-understand glossary of watch terms to assist you in acquiring a better understanding of timepiece mechanics. Understanding the DNA of a watch can help you make informed decisions when it comes to choosing the right timepiece for you. Whether you are looking for a simple, classic design or a more complex, feature-packed watch, understanding the DNA of a watch can help you find the perfect match for your style and needs. BASIC FUNCTIONALITY The main spring is a source of stored energy, and the oscillating escapement assembly regulates the rate at which that energy is released. Because the gears in the “wheel train,” which consists of the mainspring and escapement, move at various speeds, the watch is able to “tell time.” MAINSPRING TRAIN OF WHEELS BALANCE WHEEL BLOG TO READ – WATCH MOVEMENTS 101 CAN’T TELL A QUARTZ FROM AN AUTOMATIC? HERE’S WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW A watch has many parts, but the most important of them by far is its movement. A movement is composed of many pieces (hundreds in some cases), and it is the driving force behind your watch, moving the hands and keeping time accurately day in, and day out. While watch technology has been eclipsed by smartphones and other digital devices in recent years, it’s still pretty fascinating when you get down to it – especially considering that people were making accurate watches long before they could design them on computers. Not all movements, however, are alike. Discover how to differentiate between the most common kinds and appreciate the merits of each. READ MORE , Written by Jeremy Freed TIME SETTING & WINDING Adjusting the time requires pulling out the crown and winding the watch requires pushing it in. Depending on whether you're pulling out or pushing in, a separate set of gears will be engaged. HANDS In conjunction with a dial, hands indicate what time it is. Hands indicate the hours, minutes, and frequently the seconds. Hands come in a variety of styles from simple batons, swords, and pointers, to ornate designs with curves and colors. CROWN The crown allows the watch to be manually wound when required, when pulled out it allows the time to be set. Watches with a date function often have a middle position between the winding position and the time setting position which allows the date to be set as well. Waterproof watches often have a screw-down crown to prevent it from being pulled out while being exposed to water. One Day. One Global Community.” On October 10, the world will celebrate World Watch Day, a day dedicated to the art, culture, and passion of watchmaking. From seasoned collectors to first-time enthusiasts, from master artisans to curious newcomers, this is a global invitation to pause, connect, and share in the timeless beauty of horology. Through local gatherings, online events, inspiring stories, and wrist shots shared across the globe, World Watch Day will highlight how watches are far more than instruments of timekeeping; they are symbols of heritage, innovation, and human creativity. Join the movement, discover the stories behind the timepieces, and be part of a worldwide celebration where every second counts. #WorldWatchDay ARTICLES ABOUT WORLD WATCH DAY WORLD WATCH DAY — WHY CELEBRATE TIMEPIECES? pages.content: RedBar – WatchDNA Skip to content BUILDING A COMMUNITY OF WATCH LOVERS WE AIM TO BECOME YOUR TRUSTED DIRECTORY FOR ALL THINGS WATCHES Item added to your cart View cart Check out Continue shopping RedBar Redbar, a globally recognized watch enthusiast group, began as a modest gathering in New York City and has evolved into an international community of horology lovers. Founded by Adam Craniotes , @Craniotes , and a few dedicated watch enthusiasts, Redbar's mission was to create a judgment-free, pretentious-free space for people to bond over their shared love for watches, enjoy drinks, and form lasting friendships. Initially, small, informal meetings were held at a bar that gave the group its name. Over time, the gatherings grew, and with the help of social media, Redbar's popularity soared, attracting members from various walks of life. Today, Redbar NYC meets monthly, inspiring the formation of chapters in cities like Toronto, London, Bombay, Edinburgh, and Dallas/Fort Worth. With over 85 chapters worldwide, Redbar continues to maintain its core values of inclusivity, camaraderie, and a shared passion for horology, offering a welcoming space for both seasoned collectors and new enthusiasts to explore the world of watches. These collaborations are built on trust and shared values, as we continue working alongside media partners who help educate, inform, and inspire the global watch community. ‹ SOVRYGN WATCHES: A JOURNEY OF PASSION AND PERSEVERANCE February 24, 2026 GEORGE SULLY: FROM CANADIAN DESIGN ICON TO ADVOCATE AND STORYTELLER January 25, 2026 › Dedicated Watch Media Dedicated watch media are specialized publications focused exclusively on horology. They provide in-depth technical analysis, historical context, hands-on reviews, and industry insights tailored to collectors and enthusiasts. These outlets often shape collector opinion, influence brand perception, and serve as authoritative voices within the global watch community. A Timely Perspective A blog to watch Escapement Magazine Fratello Watches Hodinkee Monochrome Neue Uhren🇩🇪 Orafix🇮🇹 Passion horlogère🇫🇷 Quill & Pad Revolution Swiss Watches Magazine The Calibrated Wrist Time + Tide Time Square Magazine🇹🇼 Watch Collecting Lifestyle Watch Journal Watchonista Watch Pro Watch Time Watch Your Time Worn & Wound Wrist Enthusiast Lifestyle & fashion media Lifestyle & fashion media approach watches through the lens of design, style, and elevated living. Coverage emphasizes aesthetics, craftsmanship, brand storytelling, celebrity associations, and cultural relevance, positioning timepieces as symbols of achievement, refined taste, and personal identity. Rather than focusing purely on technical specifications, these outlets frame watches within the broader world of wealth, travel, automobiles, and fine living. Their readership—often composed of high-net-worth individuals and aspirational consumers—makes them powerful platforms for brand elevation, desirability, and lifestyle-driven visibility. BDC Magazine Collection Magazine Elle Elle Canada Elle Quebec🇫🇷 Fairmont Magazine Fashion Magazine Gear Patrol Globe & Mail Style magazine Glory Sports GQ Haute Living Hollywood Reporter Nuvo Magazine Robb Report Sharp Magazine The Kit Financial & Business Media Financial and business media analyze watches as economic assets and strategic industry players. Coverage may include market performance, brand acquisitions, resale value trends, and investment potential. These platforms position watches within the global luxury economy and appeal to executives, investors, and business-minded readers. Forbes Trade & Industry Media (B2B) Trade and industry publications serve professionals within the watch and jewelry ecosystem, including brands, authorized dealers, distributors, and suppliers. Their content focuses on retail strategy, distribution networks, product launches, trade shows, and regulatory developments. These outlets are essential for B2B visibility and industry credibility. pages.content: brands by Group – WatchDNA Skip to content BUILDING A COMMUNITY OF WATCH LOVERS WE AIM TO BECOME YOUR TRUSTED DIRECTORY FOR ALL THINGS WATCHES Item added to your cart View cart Check out Continue shopping DISCOVER ALL WATCH GROUPS At WatchDNA, our mission is to Educate , Entertain , and Connect the global watch community. One of the first insights we uncovered is that many people don’t realize most watch brands belong to larger watch groups—each uniting a range of brands, resources, and horological expertise under one roof. This page is designed to help you explore these groups and understand their influence on the industry. We hope you learn something new—and enjoy the journey through the world of watches. CASIO COMPUTER CO. LTD Casio, founded in 1946, became a digital watch pioneer with the 1974 CASIOTRON and cemented its legacy through iconic innovations like G-Shock, Pro Trek, Edifice, and the luxury-focused Oceanus. CITIZEN WATCH CO. LTD Citizen Watch Co. Ltd., founded in 1918, has grown into a global horological powerhouse known for Eco-Drive technology and innovation, with a diverse portfolio that includes Citizen, Bulova, Accutron, Frederique Constant, Alpina, Campanola, and Arnold & Son. COMPAGNIE FINANCIÈRE RICHEMONT SA Compagnie Financière Richemont SA, founded in 1988 by Johann Rupert, is a global luxury powerhouse that owns a diverse portfolio of historic watch and jewelry Maisons, including Cartier , Vacheron Constantin , and IWC , and is renowned for preserving craftsmanship while embracing innovation. FESTINA GROUP The Festina Group , a Spanish watch conglomerate founded in 1984, owns and operates several global watch brands—including Festina , Jaguar , Candino , Lotus , and Calypso —offering a range of Swiss-made and fashion-forward timepieces across various price points, with a strong heritage in sports marketing and cycling. FOSSIL GROUP INC The Fossil Group, founded in 1984, has grown into one of the world’s largest watch manufacturers, producing both in-house and licensed timepieces for brands like Armani Exchange , Diesel , DKNY , Kate Spade , Michele , Zodiac , and Skagen , each representing a unique design identity and market segment. FRANCK MULLER GROUP The Franck Muller Group, though small in size, is a respected Swiss watchmaking powerhouse that includes Franck Muller, Pierre Kunz, and Backes & Strauss, each known for bold designs, technical mastery, and distinctive identities. INVICTA WATCH GROUP Invicta Watch Group traces its origins to 1837, when Raphael Picard founded the company in La Chaux-de-Fonds, Switzerland. Originally dedicated to crafting high-quality mechanical watches at accessible prices, the brand’s name— Invicta , meaning “invincible” in Latin—has long represented resilience and innovation in watchmaking. LVMH | Louis Vuitton Moët Hennessy LVMH, while best known for fashion, champagne, and spirits, has become a major force in watchmaking through its ownership of innovative and iconic brands like TAG Heuer , Tiffany & Co , Hublot , Zenith , Bulgari , Chaumet , and Daniel Roth . MONDAINE GROUP The Mondaine Group is a family-owned Swiss watchmaker that oversees four distinctive brands—Mondaine, Luminox, Pierre Cardin, and M-Watch—each tailored to different audiences, from iconic rail-inspired designs to rugged tactical gear and affordable fashion watches. MOVADO GROUP INC. The Movado Group Inc. is a leading global watch company that strategically combines heritage and fashion through its owned br

pages.content: Platforms – WatchDNA Skip to content BUILDING A COMMUNITY OF WATCH LOVERS WE AIM TO BECOME YOUR TRUSTED DIRECTORY FOR ALL THINGS WATCHES Item added to your cart View cart Check out Continue shopping EXPLORE WATCH EDUCATION PLATFORMS Across the global watchmaking landscape, numerous independent platforms are dedicated to educating, documenting, and preserving horological knowledge. These resources offer valuable insights into watch history, craftsmanship, technical skills, and industry standards, helping both enthusiasts and professionals expand their understanding of the craft. This page highlights key educational platforms that contribute to the growth of the watch community by fostering learning, promoting transparency, and making expert information accessible worldwide. Switzerland HOROPEDIA Horopedia is a free, independent online video encyclopaedia dedicated to the art, science and culture of watchmaking, governed by the Horopedia Foundation , a Swiss public-interest non-profit organisation based in Geneva. The platform aims to preserve, document and share horological knowledge — from technical skills and historical context to core principles and professional practice — with students, professionals and enthusiasts worldwide. SWISS PRODIG Swiss Prodig is a professional Swiss industrial directory and B2B networking platform designed to connect manufacturers, subcontractors and service providers across high-precision sectors such as horology, micro-mechanics, MedTech, aerospace, electronics and industrial engineering. The platform allows companies to showcase their profiles, compare capabilities and engage directly with qualified partners for collaborative projects. HOROPEDIA The mission of the Horopedia Foundation includes educating and supporting understanding of watchmaking through rich multimedia content; producing and curating over 100 multilingual documentary films on museums, watchmaking schools, tools, components and mechanical principles; and maintaining nearly 1,000 detailed glossary pages explaining technical terms, mechanisms and historical developments. Content is provided in French, English and German. Learn more SWISS PRODIG The platform is operated by Swiss Prodig Sàrl, a limited liability company headquartered in La Chaux-de-Fonds, Switzerland, with a business focus on creating, managing and developing a digital ecosystem for industrial supply-chain visibility and collaboration. Swiss Prodig’s mission is to make the Swiss industrial and subcontracting landscape more transparent, efficient and accessible by curating a reliable, manually verified directory of companies and by offering advanced search filters, comparison tools and direct contact functionalities that streamline partner identification for buyers and project managers. Learn more 1 OF 2 While not all groups are currently featured on our site, our directory is continually evolving as we expand and update our listings. Please feel free to reach out if you would like your organization to be listed, or if you are a member of an organization that supports industry stakeholders. pages.content: Committee – WatchDNA Skip to content BUILDING A COMMUNITY OF WATCH LOVERS WE AIM TO BECOME YOUR TRUSTED DIRECTORY FOR ALL THINGS WATCHES Item added to your cart View cart Check out Continue shopping DISCOVER WATCH INDUSTRY COMMITTEES Within the global watchmaking ecosystem, several committees, associations, and organizations play a vital role in supporting local brands and industry stakeholders. These institutions help companies connect within their own countries and internationally, participate in global trade shows, strengthen industry visibility, and promote national craftsmanship on the world stage. This page introduces some of the key committees and associations dedicated to empowering watch brands and fostering cross-border collaboration. Canada CANADIAN JEWELLERS ASSOCIATION Canadian Jewellers Association (CJA) is the national trade association representing the Canadian jewellery and watch industry. Its origins date back to the early 20th century, when professionals in the field sought a unified voice to strengthen and protect the trade in Canada. CANADIAN JEWELLERY GROUP Canadian Jewellery Group (CJG) is Canada’s largest jewellery buying cooperative, empowering independent jewellers through collective purchasing and shared resources since its establishment in 1977. The organization was formed by seven jewellers from northern Ontario who collaborated to improve their purchasing power and share the benefits of negotiated supplier terms, rebates, and discounts with members across the country. CANADIAN JEWELLERS ASSOCIATION The organization traces its roots to the Jewellers Security Alliance of Canada, founded in 1884 to help improve security practices and combat theft. This initiative later evolved into the Canadian National Jewellers Association, which held its first official meeting on September 25, 1918 . By 1921, the Association had introduced its first Code of Ethics, and in 1922 it formally adopted the name Canadian Jewellers Association (CJA) . Since then, the CJA has remained committed to education, advocacy, and promoting ethical business standards across the jewellery and watch sectors. The CJA is recognized as a key authority within the Canadian jewellery and watch trade, guiding members through its Code of Ethics, educational programs, and advocacy efforts. Its work supports industry growth, strengthens consumer confidence, and ensures best practices across all areas of the jewellery sector. Learn more CANADIAN JEWELLERY GROUP Over the decades, the CJG has grown into a nationally recognized group with retail members operating numerous jewellery stores throughout Canada. By harnessing the collective buying strength of its members, the Group provides access to premium products from North America’s top suppliers while allowing independent retailers to maintain their unique identities and local presence. The organization negotiates favorable terms and conditions with endorsed suppliers and passes 100% of those advantages on to its members, including volume rebates, exclusive payment options, and centralized billing handled by the CJG head office. It also hosts an annual buying show and facilitates networking, education, and collaboration opportunities for its membership, reinforcing the competitive position of independent jewellers in the national market. Learn more 1 OF 2 France FRANCECLAT Francéclat is the professional committee dedicated to supporting and promoting France’s watchmaking, jewelry, and tableware industries—

pages.title:
MAISON BOANTON HÉRITAGE: THE ELEGANCE OF SPEED REINTERPRETED
 – WatchDNA
pages.content: MAISON BOANTON HÉRITAGE: THE ELEGANCE OF SPEED REINTERPRETED – WatchDNA Skip to content BUILDING A COMMUNITY OF WATCH LOVERS WE AIM TO BECOME YOUR TRUSTED DIRECTORY FOR ALL THINGS WATCHES Item added to your cart View cart Check out Continue shopping The Héritage by Maison Boanton is a timepiece born from emotion and mechanical memory. Inspired by iconic French automotive design, it translates purity of line and technical boldness into a watch that feels balanced, refined, and thoughtfully executed. Exterior design: lines in motion The cushion-shaped case, crafted from 316L stainless steel, measures 40 mm in diameter and 10.9 mm in thickness including the double-domed sapphire crystal with anti-reflective coating. Its proportions are designed to deliver presence without excess, while the 44.60 mm lug to lug distance ensures everyday comfort on the wrist. The domed bezel reflects light with energy, recalling polished surfaces shaped for speed. A fluted crown adds a classic and functional detail, and the screw-down caseback with sapphire exhibition window reveals the mechanics within. A dial with depth and character The blue Héritage dial is one of its defining features. Its rich and glossy finish evokes freshly polished bodywork, creating subtle variations depending on the light. The brushed racing-style hands provide excellent legibility and a discreet nod to vintage sports cars. The display remains clean and direct, true to the philosophy of honest design that defines Maison Boanton . The Alcantara strap with blue stitching completes the composition. Lightweight, durable, and pleasant to the touch, it strengthens the connection to the automotive world while adding contrast without disrupting the overall harmony. Swiss mechanics: precision and autonomy Inside beats the automatic La Joux-Perret G100 calibre, developed, assembled, and regulated in La Chaux-de-Fonds, Switzerland. It operates at 4 Hz and delivers an impressive 68-hour power reserve, ideal for those who rotate their watches over the weekend. Through the sapphire caseback, one can admire Côtes de Genève finishing, diamond-cut bevels, rhodium-plated screws heat-blued to perfection, and a solid tungsten rotor engraved and lacquered with the Maison Boanton name and logo. It is a robust movement that combines reliability with refined execution. A limited edition with a clear identity The Héritage is limited to 252 individually numbered pieces. With 50 meters of water resistance and straightforward hours, minutes, and seconds functions, it focuses on essential watchmaking values and visual balance. As an independent brand, Maison Boanton builds its identity on a clear aesthetic heritage inspired by eras when beauty and craftsmanship were inseparable. The Héritage is more than a watch influenced by classic motoring. It is a statement of intent. It captures lines, passion, and precision in a piece designed for everyday wear. Discreet yet distinctive, it stands as a contemporary tribute to legends that once moved fast and still move us today. pages.title:
THE POETRY OF THE MOON ON YOUR WRIST: RADO CENTRIX MOONPHASE
 – WatchDNA
pages.content: THE POETRY OF THE MOON ON YOUR WRIST: RADO CENTRIX MOONPHASE – WatchDNA Skip to content BUILDING A COMMUNITY OF WATCH LOVERS WE AIM TO BECOME YOUR TRUSTED DIRECTORY FOR ALL THINGS WATCHES Item added to your cart View cart Check out Continue shopping There is something hypnotic about moonlight reflecting on the horizon. That calm presence, that subtle and almost secret glow, defines the Centrix Moonphase. This timepiece transforms timekeeping into a visual experience filled with symbolism and contemporary elegance. Three Versions, One Harmony The collection is presented in three tones: brown, black, and white. Each version carries its own personality, yet all share a refined and cohesive aesthetic. The mother of pearl dials create shifting reflections depending on the light, adding depth and sophistication. The PVD finished case and high tech ceramic links establish a modern and elegant contrast. Twelve Top Wesselton diamonds mark the hours, evoking stars scattered across a night sky and reinforcing the lunar concept behind the design. A Limited Edition with Its Own Narrative The limited edition elevates the poetic character of the watch even further. It features a representation of the Earth in mother of pearl, a horizon set with 29 diamonds, and a starry sky crafted in aventurine. With only 1,001 pieces produced, this edition draws inspiration from One Thousand and One Nights and conveys a sense of fantasy, mystery, and endless possibility. Technical Specifications Explained The case is crafted from stainless steel, a material known for its durability, resistance, and timeless appeal. It ensures structural strength while maintaining a refined presence on the wrist. With a diameter of 35 millimeters, the watch offers balanced proportions. This size provides elegance and comfort, making it suitable for daily wear without feeling overwhelming. Inside, it is powered by a quartz movement, caliber R084. Quartz movements are valued for their precision and reliability, requiring minimal maintenance while delivering consistent accuracy. The total weight of 95.4 grams provides a reassuring sense of quality while remaining comfortable throughout the day. The Centrix Moonphase does more than display the time. It interprets it through the quiet glow of the moon, merging technical precision with poetic design. pages.title:
PILOTS, SAILORS, SURVEYORS: PROFESSIONS THAT QUIETLY DICTATED WATCH DE
 – WatchDNA
pages.content: PILOTS, SAILORS, SURVEYORS: PROFESSIONS THAT QUIETLY DICTATED WATCH DE – WatchDNA Skip to content BUILDING A COMMUNITY OF WATCH LOVERS WE AIM TO BECOME YOUR TRUSTED DIRECTORY FOR ALL THINGS WATCHES Item added to your cart View cart Check out Continue shopping There’s a popular myth that watches evolved out of luxury. That branding came first, followed by heritage, followed by desire. In reality, it unfolded in reverse. Watches weren’t conceived as objects of aspiration; they emerged as solutions. Before a wristwatch became shorthand for taste or success, it was a working instrument. Certain professions, pilots, sailors, surveyors, pushed watchmaking forward through necessity rather than ambition. The changes they demanded still shape what ends up on wrists today. No hype cycles. No influencer campaigns. Just people who needed the time to be right, because getting it wrong came with consequences. Let’s rewind. Pilots: Designing for Speed, Stress, and Sky Aviation reshaped how humans related to time. Early pilots weren’t cruising at altitude behind glass cockpits and automation. They flew low, navigated by landmarks, unfolded paper maps mid-flight, and trusted instinct as much as instrumentation. Under those conditions, a watch stopped being a decoration and became part of the navigation system. Legibility mattered. So did immediacy. A pilot needed to glance at their wrist and understand the information instantly, sometimes in poor light, sometimes through vibration, often while wearing gloves. What Pilots Needed Clear, immediate legibility Precise synchronization Tools for calculating speed, distance, and fuel Reliability across shock and temperature swings That checklist became a blueprint. Oversized numerals, high-contrast dials, generous proportions, the visual language of pilot watches today traces directly back to moments when seconds mattered in the air. Pilot Watch Timeline 1904 – Wristwatches enter aviation when Alberto Santos-Dumont commissions Cartier to solve the pocket-watch problem in flight 1930s – Synchronization and hacking seconds become essential for navigation 1940s – Military aviation formalizes specifications around legibility and durability 1950s – Slide-rule bezels turn watches into wrist-mounted flight computers Iconic Pilot Watches Cartier Santos: Among the earliest wristwatches designed with purpose in mind. Its flat profile, exposed screws, and clarity were functional decisions long before they became design cues. Longines Weems Second-Setting Watch: Built around synchronization rather than self-expression. It marks the moment accuracy became shared rather than personal. Breitling Navitimer: The slide-rule bezel served a clear purpose: calculations mid-flight, without removing hands from the controls. IWC Big Pilot: Everything about it favours immediacy, scale, crown, typography. It’s designed to be understood at a glance, nothing more, nothing less. Sailors: Time as Geography On land, time tells you when. At sea, it tells you where. Latitude could be calculated with relative ease. Longitude could not. Without accurate timekeeping, ships drifted, sometimes catastrophically, off course. Precision wasn’t a refinement; it was a safeguard. What Sailors Needed Accuracy sustained over long durations Resistance to moisture and salt Construction that could endure constant motion Timing tools for navigation and racing Marine chronometers solved part of the problem, but they lived below deck. Translating that reliability

pages.title:
WATCH COLLECTING
 – WatchDNA
pages.content: WATCH COLLECTING – WatchDNA Skip to content BUILDING A COMMUNITY OF WATCH LOVERS WE AIM TO BECOME YOUR TRUSTED DIRECTORY FOR ALL THINGS WATCHES Item added to your cart View cart Check out Continue shopping The reasons for collecting are on the surface very obvious. Collectors will tell you they appreciate the engineering of movements , the artistry of dials , the objects themselves and the sum of there parts , as any collector does, be it cars, paintings or even decorative stamps. Omega I have questioned however the deeper connection to the hobby , what makes us buy in multiples ? One simple three hander should be enough. To answer this I looked at my own collection. The pieces which I won’t part with are sentimental, gifted , or have some emotional bond attached to them from how they came to me. Formex I explored then ego, are some pieces there to impress others? , or do I genuinely appreciate them as watches too rather than a status symbol. Then there are a few pieces which are off piste in design, statements I think. I sometimes wonder if these are bought to impress other collectors or for the reaction they glean. I take it back to the core of my collecting. The dopamine surge of the find and then the purchase. Are we all fulfilling a basic hormonal excitement by getting a new piece. Girard Perregaux This can fade when a piece is unboxed. It loses its shine , its excitement and spark it gives you and even gets sold on occasion. I have questioned the sanity of having a large number in the collection and still trying to acquire more and even the selfishness of the financial commitment depending on your life circumstances. Tudor I thought about all these things which appeared to be negative to me and came to the simple conclusion, we do because we enjoy and it gives us pleasure. In a life that can be very short surely that’s the point.  – WatchDNA
pages.content: BERNEY PANDA: THE PERFECT UNION OF WATCHMAKING AND JEWELRY – WatchDNA Skip to content BUILDING A COMMUNITY OF WATCH LOVERS WE AIM TO BECOME YOUR TRUSTED DIRECTORY FOR ALL THINGS WATCHES Item added to your cart View cart Check out Continue shopping Swiss Tradition and Creativity The Swiss house Berney , with over five decades of history, presents its most iconic creation: the Panda, a ring-watch that celebrates the balance between watchmaking precision and the art of jewelry. Born in the family workshop in L’Abbaye, this piece reflects the brand’s philosophy: combining mechanical tradition with creative innovation, always under the 100% Swiss Made seal. Exceptional Design and Details The Panda is presented in an oval ring measuring 16 mm in diameter and 6 mm thick, crafted in 750 white gold. The panda figure comes to life thanks to Grand Feu enamel, fired at 800°C multiple times to achieve a deep, luminous black, while its polished onyx eyes capture light in an almost magical way. A translucent bamboo branch adds a poetic touch. The ring-watch is set with 128 diamonds totaling 1.069 carats (F-G VS quality) and houses an ETA 901.001 quartz movement, with a two-year battery life, combining Swiss precision and elegance in a compact design just 6 mm thick. Craftsmanship and Excellence Each Panda requires three to five months of work, combining lost-wax casting, enameling and stone setting techniques with a final assembly that fits the movement into a very small space. More than an accessory, this ring-watch is a manifesto of watchmaking and jewelry creativity, a tribute to elegance and the family tradition that defines Berney . JCK AND LUXURY STRENGTHEN THE TIMEPIECES DESTINATION WITH STRATEGIC PA
 – WatchDNA
pages.content: JCK AND LUXURY STRENGTHEN THE TIMEPIECES DESTINATION WITH STRATEGIC PA – WatchDNA Skip to content BUILDING A COMMUNITY OF WATCH LOVERS WE AIM TO BECOME YOUR TRUSTED DIRECTORY FOR ALL THINGS WATCHES Item added to your cart View cart Check out Continue shopping The watch industry will have a new epicenter in Las Vegas in 2026. JCK and Luxury are strengthening their Timepieces initiative through key partnerships that elevate the experience for brands, retailers, and collectors during their events taking place May 29 to June 1, 2026, with invitation-only access on May 27 and 28. After announcing the expansion of the watch category, JCK and Luxury are consolidating this destination through collaborations with the Fondation Haute Horlogerie, RedBar Group, and the American Watchmakers-Clockmakers Institute, integrating education, networking, and hands-on experiences into the show. An Exclusive Destination for Watchmaking in Las Vegas Timepieces at JCK and Luxury continues to position itself as a strategic meeting point for the industry. The space will feature a carefully curated selection of watch brands that have chosen the event as their exclusive Las Vegas showcase. Exhibiting brands include Citizen Watch US , Frederique Constant , Alpina Watches , Accutron , Bulova , Movado Group , G-SHOCK , CASIO , Fossil Group , among others. Watch Education at the Core The participation of the Fondation Haute Horlogerie represents a key move for JCK and Luxury . Recognized for promoting watchmaking culture and artistry internationally, the foundation will bring educational resources, specialized content, and practical experiences aimed at deepening technical and cultural understanding behind each timepiece. This collaboration reinforces JCK 's mission to provide a comprehensive experience for watch buyers in North America, raising the educational standard within the show. Community and Collecting at the Center of the Experience Another highlight will be the global meet-up hosted by RedBar Group , considered the world’s largest watch enthusiast community. This invitation-only event will bring collectors together with some of the most recognized and innovative brands in the sector, offering access to exclusive releases and direct conversations with industry leaders. The initiative aims to strengthen the sense of community and celebrate the shared passion for fine watchmaking within the framework of JCK and Luxury . Hot 100: Honoring Industry Leaders On Friday, May 29, the exclusive Hot 100 event will take place at the Venetian Expo. This prestigious invitation-only gathering will recognize the 100 most influential figures in the American watch industry. The evening will bring together approximately 200 key executives, including retailers, brands, media, and influencers, for a night dedicated to networking, celebrating achievements, and strengthening strategic relationships within the sector. The return of the Hot 100 to Las Vegas further reinforces the relevance of JCK and Luxury as a central platform for recognition and connection among the leading players in the North American watch market. Technical Training with Hands-On Experience The American Watchmakers-Clockmakers Institute will once again bring its Mobile Classroom to the event, offering hands-on workshops and educational sessions focused on watch repair and maintenance. CASIO COMPUTER CO. History ARTICLES Casio Computer Co. was founded in 1946 by Tadao Kashio as a calculator manufacturing company and entered the timepiece market in 1974 with the release of the CASIOTRON, their first digital quartz watch. This innovative device could display hours, minutes, seconds, and the date, marking Casio’s emergence as a significant player in the digital watch industry, a place it continues to uphold today. In 1983, Casio revolutionized the watch market with the introduction of the best-selling G-Shock series and continued to innovate in the following decades with watch lines including the Pro Trek series for outdoor enthusiasts, featuring altimeters, barometers, and compasses. The company also developed the Edifice line of analog watches, combining sophisticated styling with advanced technology. Today, Casio remains one of the world’s biggest manufacturers of digital watches, with the G-Shock line alone having sold over 100 million units globally. It continues to innovate with new features like Bluetooth connectivity and solar power while revisiting classic designs for re-issues and anniversary editions. Brands summary: Casio’s greatest creation, The G-Shock line, launched in 1983, revolutionized digital watches with its unique shock-resistant design and rugged aesthetic, establishing a new category in the watch market. It continues to enjoy massive popularity worldwide and now includes dozens of models from simple retro styles to luxurious all-metal versions. Casio 's main watch line, meanwhile, offers affordable, reliable timepieces ranging from simple digital watches like the iconic A158W to more feature-rich models at accessible price points. Oceanus , Casio’s luxury division, is distinguished by watches that combine high-end materials with advanced technology. LTD CITIZEN WATCH CO. LTD History ARTICLES Citizen Watch Co. Ltd. was established by Kamekichi Yamazaki in 1918 as the Shokosha Watch Research Institute and produced its first timepiece — a pocket watch — in 1924. The name Citizen was chosen in 1930 to reflect the brand’s goal of making reliable watches at affordable prices, a legacy it continues to uphold a century later. Citizen produced Japan's first shock-resistant watch, the Parashock, in 1956, and Japan's first water-resistant watch, the Parawater, in 1959. The company’s biggest breakthrough came in 1976 with the introduction of Eco-Drive, a technology that converts both natural and artificial light into energy to power watches, eliminating the need for battery replacement. Throughout the 1980s and 1990s, Citizen continued to innovate with the development of radio-controlled watches and titanium cases while refining their Eco-Drive technology. The company expanded globally through strategic acquisitions, purchasing American watch company Bulova in 2008, Swiss movement manufacturer La Joux-Perret in 2012, and Swiss watch manufacturer Frederique Constant in 2016. Today, Citizen remains one of the world's largest watch manufacturers, and is respected equally for mechanical watches like the Tsuyosa and a wide range of Eco-Drive models. Brands summary: Citizen is known for its affordable, innovative timepieces along with their advances in Eco-Drive technology, Super Titanium, and satellite timekeeping. Its portfolio of brands, including Frederique Constant , Bulova , Alpina , Arnold & Son , Campanola and Accutron represents a wide variety of forms, functions, and historical legacies. At the high end is Frederique Constant , which was founded in 1988, and is known for offering Swiss timepieces with in-house movements — including, in recent years, a tourbillon — at relatively accessible prices. Alpina likewise brings Swiss-made credentials to the table with a line of professional outdoor timepieces characterized by their "4" antimagnetic, anti-shock, water-resistant, and stainless steel standards. The addition of American watchmaker Bulova , best known for its ties to NASA and its high-frequency movements, as well as its former sub-brand Accutron , which was relaunched independently in 2020, add depth and variety to the Citizen Group’s. With the acquisition of Manufacture La Joux-Perret, a respected Swiss movement-maker, Citizen Group also gained control of Arnold & Son , an historic English brand specializing in high-end complications. Introduced by Bulova in 1960, Accutron revolutionized watchmaking with the introduction of the first fully electronic watch. Its groundbreaking technology, the tuning fork movement, allowed for unprecedented accuracy by using a resonating tuning fork instead of the traditional mechanical movement found in watches of the era. This innovation not only enhanced timekeeping accuracy but also influenced the development of electronic watches moving forward. The Accutron brand quickly became synonymous with precision, innovation, and technology, capturing the attention of watch enthusiasts and professionals alike. It was so reliable that it was used in the early days of America's space program, further cementing its place in history as a symbol of American innovation and technological advancement. Today, Accutron continues to be celebrated for its historical significance and continues to innovate with new designs and technologies, blending modern advancements with its rich heritage to create timepieces that are both timeless and forward-thinking. pages.content: Alpina – WatchDNA Skip to content BUILDING A COMMUNITY OF WATCH LOVERS WE AIM TO BECOME YOUR TRUSTED DIRECTORY FOR ALL THINGS WATCHES Item added to your cart View cart Check out Continue shopping Alpina ALPINA WATCHES Official History ARTICLES Alpina is a premium watchmaking company that has been operating out of Geneva, Switzerland for more than 135 years. Recognizable by its signature red triangle, Alpina watches are made in Switzerland. Patents and innovative calibres are only two of the many firsts that Alpina, a true pioneer in the Swiss watchmaking industry, is responsible for. Alpina is a genuine trailblazer in the industry. Alpina is widely regarded as the company that first introduced the notion of a Swiss sports watch when it released its now-iconic Alpina 4 model in the year 1938. 1883 Gottlieb Hauser founds the "Alpina Union Horlogère S.A (UH) / Schweizerische Uhrmacher-Genossenschaft (SUG)" ("Corporation of Swiss Watchmakers"). His vision was the "Alpinist Principle": uniting Swiss watchmaking industry forces to accomplish the highest manufacturing quality and technological innovation under the most favourable conditions. The Union soon began manufacturing its own calibres. 1890 The Union relocates its headquarters to Biel/Bienne, where prominent member Jacob Straub operated his calibre factory (J. Straub & Co). At that time, the Geneva-based Alpina Ebauches factory, owned by Duret & Colonnaz, manufactured ébauches. 1900 The Alpina Union Horlogère takes part in the 1900 Universal Exposition in Paris and exhibits its calibres and creations to a distinguished and international audience. 1901 Jacob Straub registers the trademark "Alpina" to safeguard his previously unmarked 12- and 19-line calibres. As J. Straub & Co. is the Union's primary calibre supplier, virtually all high-end timepieces now bear the renowned "Alpina" insignia on their movements. As a result, Alpina was initially used as a protection and quality mark for exceptional movements. 1903 Gottlieb Hauser and his son undertake a perilous mission to Russia in order to establish a stronghold in the Tsarist empire. At the conclusion of their 50-day journey, their ledgers are filled with the names of a surprising number of new customers, some of whom are renowned. In 1955, Gottlieb Hauser Jr. penned "From Bienne to Astrakhan: The Adventures of a Swiss Watch Representative in Russia Fifty Years Ago" as an account of this journey. 1908 In celebration of its 25th anniversary, the Union registers the "Alpina" watch brand. For reasons of prestige, the Union members, the "Alpinists", agreed to use the brand "Alpina" only for watches that housed specified high-quality calibres, such as those with Breguet hairsprings and compensating balances. As was customary during this era, a potent name required a unique insignia; thus, the legendary Alpina red triangle emblem, the "Alpiner," was created. 1909 The Alpina Union Horlogère establishes the "Pracisions-Uhrenfabrik Alpina Glashütte i/S" in Glashütte, Germany, to manufacture watches. Geneva, Biel, Besançon (F), and Glashütte were now home to the Union's factories. 1910 The Alpina Union Horlogère produces a perpetual calendar complications for pocket watches. A significant technical obstacle for the time period. 1910 In its Glashütte facility, Alpina produces its first chronograph. In Switzerland, these timepieces were finely adjusted by W.A. The company has maintained a strong focus on traditional luxury craftsmanship while adapting to modern market demands, emphasizing the distinct identity and heritage of each Maison while providing them with shared resources and expertise. Richemont is known for its long-term approach to brand management, focusing on preserving brand value and craftsmanship rather than rapid expansion. The group continues to be one of the most influential players in the global watch market. Brands summary: There’s a lot of depth and breadth to the brands under the Richemont umbrella, from the makers of the very first wrist-worn watch (the Cartier Santos) to the world’s oldest continually operating watchmaker ( Vacheron Constantin ) to historic Maisons like Montblanc , Roger Dubuis , Piaget , Panerai , and IWC . Then, of course, there’s Germany’s leading watchmaker A. Lange & Söhne , which is prized for its movements and finishing, and produces watches on par with anything coming out of Switzerland’s finest workshops. There’s also Jaeger-LeCoultre , often called "the watchmaker's watchmaker," which combines iconic designs like the Reverso with a spectrum of traditional watchmaking crafts including enameling and miniature painting. From the accessible luxury of Baume et Mercier to the intricate and whimsical creations of Van Cleef & Arpels , Richemont’s brands are as varied as they are historically significant. Through these diverse maisons, Richemont preserves traditional watchmaking craftsmanship while embracing innovation across different luxury price segments.
